attractions.name: Ann Morrison Park
attractions.description: Ann Morrison Park is a expansive 153-acre urban park situated along the Boise River and is a prominent member of the city's "Ribbon of Jewels." The park serves as a major recreational hub, featuring diverse amenities from a iconic spray fountain and various sports fields to a dedicated off-leash dog island and access to the Boise River Greenbelt. (City of Boise, Wikipedia)
attractions.region: Downtown Boise / Ribbon of Jewels (City of Boise)
attractions.hours: Monday - Sunday: Sunrise to Sunset (Lighted ballfields remain open until 11:00 PM for scheduled games) (City of Boise)
attractions.admission: Free (City of Boise)
highlights: ['Distinctive interactive spray fountain', 'Together Treasure Valley Dog Island off-leash area', 'Direct access to the Boise River Greenbelt pathway', 'Comprehensive sports facilities for softball, soccer, cricket, and football', '20-hole disc golf course and an outdoor gym', 'Candy Cane Playground for ages 2-12']
attractions.tips: The park is the primary take-out spot for floaters on the Boise River; shuttles to Barber Park operate from mid-June through Labor Day. Dogs are permitted off-leash throughout the entire park during the winter season from November 1 to February 28. For major events like the Fourth of July fireworks, visitors are advised to bring flashlights and sunscreen. (Visit Idaho, Reddit)
attractions.nearby: Kathryn Albertson Park, Julia Davis Park (Zoo Boise, Boise Art Museum), Boise State University (Albertsons Stadium), and the Idaho Anne Frank Human Rights Memorial (Expedia, Tripadvisor)

attractions.description: Barbacoa is a multi-dimensional fine dining concept that blends a restaurant with a museum and art gallery to create a unique sensory experience. The menu features an inventive fusion of Latin, Spanish, and Caribbean flavors, famous for signature offerings like hot rock steaks and tableside guacamole. Located next to Parkcenter Park, it offers a vibrant atmosphere with stunning views and an extensive collection of imported art. Barbacoa Boise
attractions.address: 276 Bobwhite Court, Boise, Idaho 83706 Barbacoa Boise

attractions.admission: Free (No charge for table bookings) Barbacoa Boise FAQ

attractions.tips: It is highly recommended to call ahead for reservations, although there is no booking fee. Be sure to explore the various art installations throughout the building, including the wine tunnel and imported sculptures. Barbacoa Boise FAQ

Their downtown Boise location offers an intimate, casual atmosphere with over 20 rotating taps, focusing on "Old World style with New World flavor."

It is the only museum in the United States specifically focused on the heritage of the Basque people and their history in the Idaho region. (The Basque Museum & Cultural Center, Wikipedia)

attractions.name: Bittercreek Alehouse
attractions.description: Founded in 1996, Bittercreek Alehouse is a landmark in downtown Boise's craft beer scene, dedicated to serving "Good Beer" from small, independent brewers. The establishment emphasizes sustainability and community, sourcing over half of its menu from local organic producers and even maintaining an on-site worm farm for composting. With its own basement brewery and a wide-ranging pub menu, it offers a quintessential Pacific Northwest dining and drinking experience.
attractions.hours: Sunday – Wednesday: 11:00 AM – 12:00 AM; Thursday – Saturday: 11:00 AM – 1:30 AM (21+ after 10:00 PM)
attractions.days_open: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, Sunday
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: ['Extensive "Good Beer" list featuring a large, rotating selection of independent and local craft brews.', 'Locally sourced menu with 50% of ingredients coming from regional organic farmers and producers.', 'On-site basement brewery used for experimental batches and research for sister brewery Woodland Empire.', 'Family-friendly atmosphere including retro activity lunchboxes to keep children entertained.', "Large, awning-covered outdoor patio perfect for people-watching on Boise's popular 8th Street.", 'Pioneering sustainability initiatives, including an on-site vermicompost (worm farm) system for food waste.']
attractions.tips: Visit during the weekday Happy Hour (3-6 PM) for deals on indie beers and their famous Bittercreek fries. The restaurant is popular and can get noisy during peak hours, so arrive early for a quieter experience on the patio. Keep in mind the venue transitions to a 21-and-over establishment every night after 10:00 PM.

It serves as a premier destination for culinary experiences, boutique shopping, and premier entertainment, featuring a food hall, cinema, and music venue.

attractions.name: Bogus Basin Mountain Recreation Area
attractions.description: Bogus Basin Mountain Recreation Area is a 501(c)(3) non-profit year-round destination dedicated to accessible and affordable outdoor recreation. Spanning 2,600 acres, it offers extensive skiing and snowboarding in the winter, and mountain biking, hiking, and a mountain coaster in the summer. Bogus Basin, Wikipedia

attractions.name: Boise Art Museum
attractions.description: The Boise Art Museum (BAM) is a premier visual arts institution that engages the community through exceptional exhibitions, a growing permanent collection, and diverse educational programs. As the only American Alliance of Museums (AAM) accredited museum collecting fine art in Idaho, it serves as a vital cultural hub in the Treasure Valley.
attractions.hours: Tue–Sat: 10 a.m. – 5 p.m.; Sun: Noon – 5 p.m.; First Thursday: 10 a.m. – 7 p.m.; Mon: Closed.
attractions.admission: General: $9, Seniors (62+): $7, Military (active/retired): $7, Students (Grades 1-12 & College w/ ID): $5, Children 5 and under: FREE, BAM Members: FREE. Special Admissions: First Thursday is 'pay what you can' (10 a.m. – 7 p.m.); 3rd Wednesday is FREE for Seniors (62+).
highlights: ['Extensive permanent collection featuring contemporary realism, Northwest art, and modern ceramics.', "The world's largest public collection of works by acclaimed Idaho outsider artist James Charles Castle.", "Hosts the annual 'Art in the Park' festival every September, featuring over 200 artists.", 'Dedicated children’s ARTexperience Gallery and educational studios for hands-on creativity.', 'Rotating schedule of approximately 20 special and traveling exhibitions each year.', 'Beautiful outdoor sculpture garden and Art Deco/Egyptian Revival architecture.']

Visitors are encouraged to use the free 2-hour parking in Julia Davis Park or request an extended pass at the admissions desk if staying longer. Only pencils are permitted for sketching or note-taking inside the museum.

attractions.name: Boise Cascade Headquarters (Boise Plaza)
attractions.description: Originally built in 1971 as the Boise Cascade Corporation Headquarters, this iconic structure is now known as Boise Plaza. Designed by the world-renowned architectural firm Skidmore, Owings & Merrill, it was the first major downtown project approved by the Boise Redevelopment Agency. The building is celebrated for its modernist "half-cube" design and its expansive eight-story interior atrium filled with natural light and live plants. Idaho Architecture Project, SAH Archipedia
attractions.address: 1111 West Jefferson St, Suite 300, Boise, ID 83702-5389
attractions.hours: Monday: 09:00 AM - 05:00 PM, Tuesday: 09:00 AM - 05:00 PM, Wednesday: 09:00 AM - 05:00 PM, Thursday: 09:00 AM - 05:00 PM, Friday: 09:00 AM - 05:00 PM, Saturday: Closed, Sunday: Closed
attractions.admission: Free (Lobby and Atrium access)
highlights: ['Designed by legendary architecture firm Skidmore, Owings & Merrill in a Modern/New Formalism style.', 'Features a massive eight-story interior atrium with a concierge desk and lush indoor greenery.', "Home to the 'AquA' art installation by Ray King, featuring over 2,000 suspended glass cubes.", "The structure's width and scale were intentionally designed to complement the nearby Idaho State Capitol.", 'Serves as the long-standing corporate headquarters for Boise Cascade Company since its completion.', "Boise's largest office building, encompassing an entire city block in the heart of downtown."]
attractions.tips: Visitors can enter the lobby to view the impressive atrium and 'AquA' glass sculpture. Metered street parking is available on surrounding blocks, and several public parking garages are nearby. The building is just a short walk from the Idaho State Capitol, making it a convenient stop for history and architecture enthusiasts. Visit Boise
attractions.nearby: Idaho State Capitol, Cherie Buckner-Webb Park, The Grove Plaza, Boise Art Museum, Boise River Greenbelt, 8th Street Restaurant Row. Rafanelli & Nahas

The market features a wide selection of produce, meats, specialty items, and a dedicated wine shop and pet supply store (Boise Co-op Market, Wikipedia).

It is housed in a refurbished 1935 seed warehouse in the BODO District and features an intimate 231-seat black box theater that frequently showcases world premieres and staged readings. attractions.name: Boise Fry Company
attractions.description: Boise Fry Company is a unique Idaho eatery that flips the script by making fries the main dish and 'burgers on the side.' They specialize in locally grown organic potatoes prepared using a classic Belgian cooking technique, served with a variety of handcrafted sauces and salts. Guests can customize their experience by choosing from multiple potato types, cuts, and protein options including bison, wagyu-blend beef, and house-made vegan patties.

attractions.name: Camel's Back Park
attractions.description: Camel's Back Park is an 11-acre urban park located in the historic North End of Boise, Idaho, serving as a primary gateway to the Boise Foothills. It is best known for its iconic camelback-shaped hill, which offers a steep climb rewarded by stunning panoramic views of the city and surrounding landscape. The park features a variety of recreational facilities, including a universally accessible playground, tennis courts, and a specialized outdoor gym (City of Boise, Wikipedia).
attractions.hours: Sunrise to Sunset (City of Boise)
attractions.admission: Free (City of Boise)
highlights: 'Iconic camelback-shaped hill with panoramic views of downtown Boise ([Boise with Kids)', "'The Chute,' a steep sandy hill popular for climbing and sliding (KBOI)", 'Bodybuilding.com Park Gym featuring 15 pieces of outdoor fitness equipment (City of Boise)', 'Direct access to Hulls Gulch Reserve and extensive Boise Foothills trail systems (North End Boise)', 'Universally accessible playground with bonded rubber surfacing (City of Boise)', 'Four tennis courts and a sand volleyball court available for public use (City of Boise)']
attractions.tips: The climb to the top of the hill is very steep, gaining 150 feet in just 0.1 mile, so wear appropriate footwear. There is very little tree cover on the trails, so bring plenty of water and sun protection (Boise with Kids). Parking is available but can be limited during weekends and events like the Hyde Park Street Fair (North End Boise).
attractions.nearby: Hyde Park, Hulls Gulch Reserve, Boise Foothills, Military Reserve (North End Boise, Boise with Kids)

attractions.name: Capital City Public Market
attractions.description: Founded in 1994, the Capital City Public Market is Boise's premier Saturday destination, featuring over 150 rotating vendors in the heart of downtown. It serves as a vibrant hub for local artists, farmers, and food producers to connect directly with the community, celebrating Idaho's creativity and craftsmanship. (Capital City Public Market)

attractions.admission: Free (Capital City Public Market)

attractions.tips: Arrive early to secure the best selection of fresh produce and handmade items. Most street parking in the area is free but limited to 2 hours on Saturdays. The market accepts SNAP benefits, and bringing your own reusable bags is encouraged. (Capital City Public Market, Tripadvisor)

attractions.name: Cathedral of the Rockies (Boise First United Methodist Church)
attractions.description: Cathedral of the Rockies, officially known as Boise First United Methodist Church, is an affirming and inclusive congregation in historic Boise. Founded in 1872, it is the city's first Methodist church and currently features a grand Gothic Revival building completed in 1960. The church is known for its "All Means All" mission, magnificent stained-glass windows, and vibrant community arts programs Official Website, Wikipedia.

attractions.name: Chandlers Prime Steaks & Fine Seafood (Chandlers)
attractions.description: Chandlers is an upscale, locally owned dinnerhouse in downtown Boise that has specialized in prime steaks and jet-fresh seafood since 1994 (Chandlers). Located within Hotel 43, it features a sophisticated atmosphere complemented by live jazz performances every night of the week (DiRōNA). The restaurant is renowned for its extensive wine list and signature "Ten-Minute Martini" (Chandlers).

attractions.name: Foothills East Reserve
attractions.description: Foothills East Reserve is a 240-acre natural area in the Boise Foothills that offers high-desert terrain with native vegetation. It serves as a gateway to the more extensive Ridge to Rivers trail system, providing scenic vistas of the city and critical habitat for local wildlife. (City of Boise, Ridge to Rivers)

attractions.hours: Sunrise to sunset daily (City of Boise)
attractions.admission: Free (City of Boise)
highlights: 'Access to the extensive Ridge to Rivers trail network ([Ridge to Rivers)', 'Panoramic views of Downtown Boise and the Treasure Valley (Northend.org)', '240 acres of protected natural open space (City of Boise)', 'Native high-desert plant communities, including sagebrush and bitterbrush (Northend.org)', 'Opportunities for hiking, trail running, and bird watching (Visit Boise)', 'Proximity to the historic East End neighborhood (Apartments.com)']
attractions.tips: Stay on designated trails to protect the fragile desert soil and native plants. Avoid using the trails when they are muddy to prevent erosion and deep ruts. Bring plenty of water and sun protection as there is very little shade in the reserve. (Ridge to Rivers)

attractions.name: Idaho State Capitol Building
attractions.description: The Idaho State Capitol is a Renaissance Revival architectural masterpiece and the seat of Idaho's executive and legislative branches. Completed in 1920, it is famously the only state capitol in the United States heated entirely by geothermal water. The building's exterior is constructed from locally quarried sandstone, while the interior features an expansive collection of scagliola pillars and diverse marble types.
attractions.hours: Monday – Sunday: 7:00 AM – 7:00 PM (Idaho Legislature)
attractions.admission: Free (Idaho Legislature)
highlights: 'Only U.S. state capitol heated by geothermal water pumped from 3,000 feet underground ([Idaho.gov)', 'Majestic 208-foot dome topped with a five-foot-tall bronze eagle (Capitol Commission)', 'Interior features 219 pillars made of scagliola, a complex mixture of granite and marble dust (Wikipedia)', "Home to the 'Winged Victory' statue, a plaster replica of the Nike of Samothrace gifted by France (Let's Go Boise)", 'Publicly accessible full-scale replica of the Liberty Bell located at the Jefferson Street entrance (Wikipedia)', "Rotunda ceiling features 13 large stars for the original colonies and 43 small stars for Idaho's statehood (We Know Boise)"]
attractions.tips: Visitors are welcome to explore at their leisure; however, security screening is required at entrances. Self-guided tour booklets and scavenger hunts are available on the 1st floor and Garden level. While legislative sessions are in progress, the public can observe from the 4th-floor galleries (Idaho Legislature).
attractions.nearby: Basque Block, Freak Alley Gallery, Julia Davis Park, Boise Depot

attractions.name: Military Reserve (also known as Fort Boise-Military Reserve Park)
attractions.description: Military Reserve is a 734-acre natural area located in northeastern Boise, Idaho, that offers a variety of recreational activities and historic sites. It features an extensive trail system for hiking and mountain biking, a dedicated bike park, a fenced dog park, and a public archery range. The park also encompasses the historic Fort Boise Military Reserve Cemetery, where veterans from the Mexican War through the Spanish-American War are interred. (City of Boise, Ridge to Rivers)

attractions.name: Morley Nelson Snake River Birds of Prey National Conservation Area
attractions.description: The Morley Nelson Snake River Birds of Prey National Conservation Area is home to the densest population of nesting raptors in North America, and perhaps the world. Spanning 485,000 acres along 81 miles of the Snake River, it provides a unique habitat for over 800 pairs of eagles, falcons, hawks, and owls. Visitors can explore the deep canyon, desert plateau, and significant cultural sites managed by the Bureau of Land Management. (BLM, Wikipedia)
attractions.region: South of Boise, spanning Ada, Canyon, Elmore, and Owyhee counties (Wikipedia)
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours (NCA Public Lands); BLM Office: M-F, 8:00 a.m. - 4:30 p.m. (BLM)
attractions.admission: Free (Make Like An Ape Man)
highlights: 'Home to the greatest concentration of nesting birds of prey in North America ([BLM)', 'Supports approximately 800 pairs of hawks, owls, eagles, and falcons each spring (BLM)', 'Encompasses 81 miles of the dramatic Snake River Canyon and surrounding desert plateau (BLM)', 'Contains significant archaeological sites and petroglyphs at nearby Celebration Park (Wikipedia)', 'Offers the Dedication Point overlook with interpretive trails and spectacular canyon views (Visit Idaho)', 'Provides diverse recreation including hiking, mountain biking, horseback riding, and rafting (Visit Idaho)']
attractions.tips: The best time to view raptors is mid-March through June when they are nesting and young birds are learning to fly. Bring binoculars, water, and sunscreen, as the area has few public facilities and can be very hot and dry. (Visit Idaho)

attractions.name: Old Idaho Penitentiary State Historic Site
attractions.description: Built in 1870, the Old Idaho Penitentiary is one of only four territorial prisons open to the public today. During its 101 years of operation, the site housed over 13,000 inmates and witnessed numerous escapes, scandals, and the transition of Boise from a frontier town to a modern capital city (Idaho State Historical Society, Wikipedia).
attractions.hours: Summer (Memorial Day - Labor Day): Monday-Sunday, 10:00 am - 5:00 pm; Winter: Monday-Sunday, 12:00 pm - 5:00 pm. Last admission is at 4:00 pm daily (Idaho State Historical Society).

attractions.admission: Adults (13+): $8, Seniors (60+): $6, Children (6-12): $5, Children under 6: Free, Members: Free. Discounts available for AAA, students, and veterans (Idaho State Historical Society).
highlights: ['J. Curtis Earl Memorial Exhibit of historic arms and military memorabilia', 'Solitary confinement cells, including the notorious "Siberia" section', 'Cell House 5, which contains the gallows and former death row', "Idaho's Merci Train boxcar, a gift from France following WWII", 'Over 30 preserved historic buildings and cell blocks made from local sandstone']
attractions.tips: Guided tours are typically available for an additional $2 fee, while self-guided tours are included with admission. Visitors should wear comfortable walking shoes as the site is expansive and features uneven terrain. Note that professional photography requires a pre-arranged permit (Idaho State Historical Society).

attractions.name: Race to Robie Creek®
attractions.description: The Race to Robie Creek is a legendary 13.1-mile half marathon known as 'the toughest half marathon in the Northwest.' Starting at Fort Boise Park, the course climbs 2,100 feet over 8.5 miles to Aldape Summit before descending 1,700 feet to the finish line at Robie Creek Park. Participants face a mix of paved roads and unimproved dirt/gravel paths through the scenic Boise foothills.

attractions.admission: $75.00 entry fee (plus online transaction fee) for participants; Spectator Bus Pass required for shuttle to finish area.

attractions.name: Westside Drive In
attractions.description: The Westside Drive In has been a Boise staple since 1957, offering a nostalgic 50s-era dining experience with a gourmet touch. Purchased by classically trained Executive Chef Lou Aaron in 1994, the restaurant is famous for its extensive made-from-scratch menu and its iconic Idaho Ice Cream Potato. It has gained national recognition, notably being featured on the Food Network's "Diners, Drive-Ins and Dives."

attractions.tips: Don't miss the Idaho Ice Cream Potato, which is vanilla ice cream masterfully crafted to look like a baked potato. Check the daily specials schedule, such as Fisherman's Monday for deals on fish and chips or Taco Tuesday. The original State Street location offers the most authentic retro experience with its iconic neon signage. Both locations provide outdoor seating and drive-thru service for those on the go.

It is a leader in conservation, being the first zoo to integrate a conservation fee into its admission prices to fund wildlife protection projects worldwide (Zoo Boise).

attractions.name: Deer Flat National Wildlife Refuge (U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service)
attractions.description: Deer Flat National Wildlife Refuge is one of the oldest in the national system, established in 1909 as a breeding ground for migratory birds. It serves as a critical resting and wintering area for waterfowl along the Pacific Flyway and is recognized as a 'Globally Important Bird Area' (U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service, Wikipedia).
attractions.address: 13751 Upper Embankment Road, Nampa, ID 83686-8046 (U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service)
attractions.region: Canyon County / Treasure Valley (U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service)
attractions.hours: Refuge Lands: Daily during daylight hours; Visitor Center: Monday-Friday 8:00 AM – 4:00 PM, Saturday 10:00 AM – 4:00 PM; closed on Federal holidays (U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service)
attractions.days_open: Daily for refuge lands; Monday through Saturday for the Visitor Center (U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service)
attractions.admission: Free; there is no charge to visit (U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service)
highlights: 'Over 10 miles of hiking trails including the accessible Sagebrush and Swallow Nest Trails ([U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service)', 'Bird watching at Lake Lowell, home to nesting grebes and large concentrations of wintering waterfowl (U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service)', 'Recreational activities including boating, swimming (seasonal), and fishing at Lake Lowell (U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service)', 'The Snake River Islands Unit, offering a driving tour of important nesting habitats (U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service)', 'The Refuge Visitor Center featuring interactive exhibits, a KidSpace area, and a wildlife observation room (U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service)', 'Photography and seasonal hunting opportunities in designated areas (U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service)']
attractions.tips: Wildlife is most active during the morning and evening; if driving, use your car as a blind for better viewing. Visitors should bring their own water as there is no potable water on the trails, and check the sighting board at the visitor center for recent activity (U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service).

attractions.name: City of Rocks National Reserve
attractions.description: City of Rocks National Reserve is a world-renowned destination famous for its dramatic 60-story tall granite spires and monoliths. It preserves a significant segment of the California National Historic Trail, where visitors can still see pioneer signatures written in wagon axle grease. Today, it is a premier location for rock climbing, hiking, and experiencing the rugged beauty of the Old West. (National Park Service)
attractions.hours: Reserve: Open 24 hours daily. Visitor Center: 8:00 AM - 4:30 PM. (Mid-April to Mid-October: Daily; Mid-October to Mid-April: Tuesday-Saturday) (National Park Service)
attractions.days_open: Every day (Reserve); Tuesday-Saturday (Visitor Center in winter); Daily (Visitor Center in summer) (National Park Service)
attractions.admission: Free (National Park Service)
highlights: 'World-class rock climbing with over 700 developed routes on ancient granite ([National Park Service).', 'Well-preserved segments of the California National Historic Trail featuring pioneer signatures at Register Rock (Visit Idaho).', 'Over 22 miles of hiking trails winding through a "Silent City" of geological wonders (National Park Service).', 'Unique geological features such as Window Arch and the 2.5 billion-year-old granite formations (Idaho Parks and Recreation).', "Home to Idaho's only pinyon pine forest and a diverse range of high-desert wildlife (Tripadvisor)."]
attractions.tips: Cell service is extremely limited, so download maps and trail info before arriving. Potable water is primarily available at the Visitor Center; bring your own supply for the backcountry. The roads within the reserve are unpaved and can be dusty or washboarded. (Visit Idaho)
attractions.nearby: Castle Rocks State Park, Almo gateway village, Almo Hot Springs (Tripadvisor)

attractions.name: Craters of the Moon National Monument and Preserve
attractions.description: Craters of the Moon National Monument and Preserve is a vast volcanic landscape featuring lava flows, cinder cones, and sagebrush islands on Idaho's Snake River Plain. It preserves over 750,000 acres of a "weird and scenic" environment shaped by volcanic activity along the Great Rift, offering a glimpse into the earth's violent geological past. (NPS Overview)
attractions.hours: Park Entrance & Loop Road: Open 24 hours daily (Loop Road typically closed to vehicles mid-November to mid-April). Robert Limbert Visitor Center: 8:00 AM - 4:30 PM daily (standard), 8:00 AM - 6:00 PM daily (early June through Labor Day). Visitor Center closed on winter holidays. (NPS Hours)
attractions.admission: Private Vehicle: $20.00, Motorcycle: $15.00, Per Person (foot/bicycle): $10.00, Craters of the Moon Annual Pass: $35.00, America the Beautiful Pass: Free-$80.00, Commercial Sedan (1-6 seats): $25.00, Commercial Van (7-15 seats): $40.00, Commercial Motor Coach (26+ seats): $100.00, Educational Groups: Free. Note: Admission is free when the Loop Road is closed to vehicles in winter. (NPS Fees)
highlights: ['Seven-mile Loop Road scenic drive through volcanic formations', 'Steep hike up Inferno Cone for 360-degree panoramic views', 'Exploring volcanic lava tube caves like Indian Tunnel and Boy Scout Cave', 'Viewing mini-volcanoes at the Spatter Cones parking area', "Walking the Devil's Orchard trail through limber pine and lava fragments", 'Stargazing in an officially designated International Dark Sky Park']
attractions.tips: A free permit from the visitor center is required for caving to prevent the spread of White-nose Syndrome. Wear sturdy, closed-toe shoes and bring plenty of water, as the lava fields can be extremely hot and sharp. Check current road conditions before visiting in the shoulder seasons. (NPS Visitor Tips)

It draws thousands of spectators to the streets of downtown McCall to participate in the traditional Mardi Gras practice of catching beads and trinkets in a celebration of Idaho's winter season.

highlights: ['Elaborate snow and ice sculptures displayed throughout downtown McCall', 'Festive floats throwing beads, toys, and other traditional Mardi Gras trinkets', 'Live musical performances and street entertainers along the parade route', 'Spectacular fireworks display launched over the frozen Payette Lake in the evening', 'Family-oriented activities such as the quirky "Monster Dog Pull" sled competition', 'Abundant local food vendors and artisan craft booths at the carnival hub']
